source: Rocky Linux 'About systemd' guide - Basic overview & Development history

options: A system daemon - an init software (service manager) for Linux, running as the first process
questions.why: systemd (system daemon) is the init system/service manager, replacing traditional SysV-style init.

questions.q: Which of these was a stated purpose behind developing systemd?
options: Parallel startup of services and a better framework for service dependencies, replacing SysV init
To remove the ability to run services
questions.why: systemd aimed at parallel service startup, better dependency handling, and reduced shell overhead versus SysV.

questions.q: What is the main criticism levelled at systemd?
options: It takes over too many components and is very complex (over a million lines), violating UNIX's KISS principle
It is too small to be useful
It cannot start services in parallel
questions.why: Critics say systemd absorbs too much and is too complex, against 'Keep It Simple, Stupid', enlarging the attack surface.

questions.q: Which macOS technology inspired many of systemd's concepts?
questions.why: Many systemd designs are inspired by Apple's launchd.

questions.q: Who created systemd and roughly when?
options: Two Red Hat engineers, Lennart Poettering and Kay Sievers, around 2010
questions.why: Poettering and Sievers (Red Hat) built the first version around 2010; Fedora 15 (2011) was the first distro to enable it by default.
